content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 9, 2007

Morton S. Park, Esq.
Lounsbery Ferguson Altona & Peak, LLP
960 Canterbury Place, Suite 300
Escondido, California 92025

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-227

Dear Mr. Park:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Seth Cohen regarding the honoraria provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that this letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; section 83114.)

QUESTIONS

1. Do the honoraria provisions of the Act prohibit Seth Cohen, a planning commissioner with the City of San Marcos, from accepting payments for speaking engagements concerning his areas of knowledge as a professor at the University of California San Diego ("USCD")?

2. Do the honoraria provisions prevent Commissioner Cohen from accepting payments for travel expenses related to the above speaking engagements?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The honoraria prohibition does not apply to speeches provided customarily, and in connection with, the practice of a bona fide profession such as teaching. Therefore, if Commissioner Cohen's speaking engagements qualify as personal

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No. I-06-227
Page No. 2

services customarily provided in connection with his profession as a university
professor, then the honoraria prohibitions would not apply. However, the
"earned income" exception to the honoraria ban does not apply if the
predominant activity of the profession is making speeches.

2. With regard to payments for travel expenses related to the speaking
engagements, you have not provided any facts or specific details regarding the
reimbursements that Commissioner Cohen would receive, such as the sources for
these payments and whether the travel is within California, within the United
States, or outside the country. The rules that apply differ depending on the actual
facts of a case. Therefore, we can only provide you with general guidance. See
discussion below.

FACTS

Your office represents the City of San Marcos. You received a request for
advice from one of your planning commissioners, Seth Cohen, regarding his receipt of
honoraria for public speaking events.

Commissioner Cohen is a full-time UCSD professor of chemistry and
biochemistry. He receives numerous invitations to speak on subjects concerning
chemistry and biochemistry. These speaking engagements are unaffiliated with UCSD;
and the organizers of these engagements offer Commissioner Cohen payments for
speaking at the events and related travel expenses.

You would like to know if it is appropriate for Commissioner Cohen to accept
these speech and travel expense payments, provided that the speaking engagements
concern his areas of knowledge as a UCSD Professor.

ANALYSIS

Honoraria

Section 89502(a) of the Government Code provides that "no elected state officer,
elected officer of a local government agency, or other individual specified in section
87200 shall accept any honorarium." As a planning commissioner with the City of San
Marcos, Seth Cohen is a local official specified in section 87200.

An honorarium is defined as any payment made in consideration for any speech
given, article published, or attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering. (Section 89501(a).) As a public official
specified in section 87200, the honorarium ban applies to Commissioner Cohen.

However, the term "honorarium" does not include earned income for personal
services which are customarily provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide

File No. I-06-227
Page No. 3

business, trade, or profession, unless the sole or predominant activity of the business,
trade, or profession is making speeches. (Sections 82030.5 and 89501(b)(1).)

"Honorarium" does not include income earned for personal services if:

(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual's business or the
individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business, trade, or
profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate,
banking, or building contracting, pursuant to California Code of Regulations,
Title 2, Sections 18932.1 through 18932.3; and

(2) The services are customarily provided in connection with the business, trade,
or profession. (Regulation 18932.)

An official is presumed to be engaged in the bona fide profession of teaching if
he or she is employed to teach at an accredited university. (Regulation 18932.2(a).)
Commissioner Cohen is a full-time professor of chemistry and biochemistry at UCSD
which is an accredited state university. Therefore, he is presumed to be engaged in the
bona fide profession of teaching.

Whether speeches and other similar activities are customarily provided with the
practice of a bona fide profession must be determined on a case-by-case basis.
(Greenwald Advice Letter, A-95-290a, copy enclosed.) You have not asked about, or
provided details about a specific engagement or event that Commissioner Cohen would
receive payment in exchange for speaking, therefore we can only provide you with
general guidance. You indicated that Commissioner Cohen receives numerous
invitations to speak on subjects concerning chemistry and biochemistry, and these
speaking engagements are unaffiliated with UCSD.

In the past, we have advised that presenting lectures and writing articles on
subject matters that are within a professor's area of expertise are generally considered
personal services that are customarily provided in connection with the practice of
teaching. (Ukman Advice Letter, No. A-99-281, copy enclosed.) Therefore, if
Commissioner Cohen's speaking engagements concern chemistry and biochemistry, his
areas of expertise as a professor at UCSD, these events could qualify as personal
services customarily provided in connection with his profession of teaching.

Nevertheless, the "earned income" exception to the honoraria ban does not apply
if the predominant activity of the profession is making speeches. (Section 89501(b)(1).)

Speechmaking is presumed to be the predominant activity of an official's profession if:

(1) The official has engaged in the profession for one year or more; and

File No. I-06-227
Page No. 4

(2) During the 12-month period before the speech and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the hours spent on the profession are devoted to,
or more than 50 percent of the gross income from the profession comes from, the
preparation or delivery of speeches. (Regulation 18932.3(a).)

We assume that making speeches is not the predominant activity of
Commissioner Cohen's profession of teaching. However, while not prohibited
honoraria, the Act requires the disclosure of the earned income.

Travel Expenses

The Act and Commission regulations provide exceptions to the honoraria
prohibition and gift limit for certain types of travel payments. (Sections 89501 and
89506; regulations 18950-18950.4.)

The term "travel payment" includes payments, advances, or reimbursements for
travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence. (Section
89501(c).)

You have not provided any details regarding reimbursement for travel expenses
that Commissioner Cohen would receive, such as the sources for these payments and
whether the travel is within California, within the United States, or outside the country.
The rules that apply differ depending on the specific facts of a case.³ Therefore, we can
only provide you with general guidance in this area. Permissible reimbursement of
travel expenses is further explained in the fact sheet "Limitations and Restrictions on
Gifts, Honoraria, Travel and Loans," and "Travel Guide for California Officials and
Candidates," which are included with this letter.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures
ER:jgl
I:\AdviceLtrs\06-227

³ For instance, regulation 18932.4 states that "honorarium" does not include "actual intrastate
transportation and any necessary lodging and subsistence provided directly in connection with [a] speech,
panel, seminar, or service, including but not limited to meals and beverages on the day of the activity.
These items are not payments and need not be reported by any filer." [Emphasis added.]
